JOB TITLE:    Wildlife Field Biologist 

 
DEPARTMENT:    Wildlife Program          

            POSITION SUMMARY:    The Wildlife Field Biologist will lead field work and day-to-day operations related to wildlife research and management projects in the western North Cascades Ecosystem. Primary emphasis will be on big game species, including: cougars, elk, deer, black bears, mountain goats, and wolves. Duties will vary by season and need, but may include executing camera traps studies, performing a variety of ground-based or aerial surveys, collecting biological samples, capturing and collaring various large mammals, and other duties as assigned. However, despite a varied workload, launching a new research project to describe cougar ecology in the area will be a primary focus. Tasks to support this new effort could include: using cage traps and/or hounds to capture and collar adult cougars; performing daily monitoring of cougar GPS data to identify clusters of locations suggesting potential cougar kill sites; investigating potential cougar kill sites to obtain prey item data; deploying and managing camera traps on kill sites; organizing collected data for analyses; and maintaining project gear and other support tasks to facilitate daily and weekly project activities. This is a full-time, regular position with: health and dental insurance; paid holiday, annual, and sick leave; and retirement plan. Employment is guaranteed for 12 months and subject to extension based on available funding. 
 SUPERVISION:
 The incumbent will join a Wildlife Biologist, Senior Wildlife Biologist, and Program Manager within the Swinomish Wildlife Program. The Senior Wildlife Biologist supervises the Wildlife Field Biologist position, while the Wildlife Biologist will also provide support and direction. The Wildlife Field Biologist may occasionally oversee junior colleagues – including Wildlife Science Technicians and Assistant Wildlife Science Technicians – as well as volunteers, in a team-oriented atmosphere to achieve Wildlife Program goals. All biologist and technician positions are directed by the Wildlife Program Manager.  
 ESSENTIAL JOB DUTIES:
 ·       Independently and collaboratively plans, coordinates, and executes a wide variety wildlife field research activities, especially as related to big game species 
·       Works independently and collaboratively with the Wildlife Biologist and Senior Wildlife Biologist to capture and collar adult cougars using cage traps  
·       Performs cougar kill site investigations to gather prey data 
·       Monitors GPS data from cougar collars on a daily and weekly basis
·       Organizes and manages biological databases using personal computers and applicable software, including: Microsoft Excel, program R, ArcGIS Pro, and other related software 
·       May perform statistical and spatial analyses on biological data using personal computers and applicable software, including: Microsoft Excel, program R, and ArcGIS
·       Engages effectively and professionally with co-managers, stakeholders, and the public as needed to achieve project goals
·       Operates and maintains field vehicles and other project equipment 
·       Supports and/or leads other wildlife research and management activities, which may include executing camera traps studies, performing a variety of ground-based or aerial surveys, collecting biological samples, and capturing and collaring various large mammals, among other tasks
·       Other duties as assigned. The specific job duties described above are intended to be primary examples of assigned tasks and not meant to be exclusive.  Employees are expected to perform any reasonable duties and tasks assigned to them by their supervisor or department director. 

 MINIMUM REQUIREMENTS AND QUALIFICATIONS
 ·       A bachelor’s degree in wildlife biology, wildlife management, biological sciences, natural resource management or closely related field 
·       At least 3 years of experience in wildlife field research, one or more years of which MUST include large carnivore capture and handling work; a master’s degree may be substituted for up to 1 year of field experience but not the large carnivore capture and handling requirement
·       Knowledge of the life history, biology, and habitat requirements of big game (cougars, elk, black-tailed deer, mountain goats, black bears, and wolves) 
·       Demonstrated physical fitness, including:  1) the ability to travel independently on foot over steep, slick, thickly-vegetated, and broken terrain safely for long distances (up to 5 miles) while carrying backpacks weighing up to 30 pounds and 2) the ability to independently lift and/or move 50-150 pounds over short distances (10-25 feet)
·       Incumbent may be required to pass a physical fitness test during the hiring process
·       Willingness and ability to provide/receive direction to/from junior/senior colleagues in an organized, professional, and safe manner in a variety of field and office situations 
·       Valid, state-issued driver’s license and/or the ability to obtain a Washington State driver’s license; must be insurable on the Tribe’s insurance policy
·       Must pass a drug test and background check
·       Must be legally allowed to carry a firearm 
·       Must either possess a Washington Department of Fish and Wildlife Hunter Education certification or be able to obtain this certification or the equivalent within 1 month of being hired (SITC will cover associated salary, travel, and course costs). Equivalent experience may be substituted and will be assessed on an individual basis. 
·       Must either possess a Wilderness First Aid certification or be able to obtain this certification or the equivalent within 2 months of being hired (SITC will cover associated salary, travel, and course costs). Equivalent experience may be substituted and will be assessed on an individual basis.
 PREFFERRED QUALIFICATIONS
 ·       A master’s degree in wildlife biology, wildlife management, biological sciences, natural resource management or closely related field that included completion of an original research thesis 
·       Five or more years of field research experience demonstrating progressive increases in responsibility culminating in senior technician-level experience or equivalent
·       Experience capturing and handling adult cougars using cage traps, snares, or hounds
 WORK ENVIRONMENT AND TIME COMMITMENT
  This is a 40 hour per week, full-time regular position.
Standard work hours at the Swinomish Indian Tribal Community are 8am to 6pm, Monday-Thursday; offices are closed on Fridays
Position duties will frequently require working outside standard work hours, including evenings and weekends 
·       Daily work outdoors in inclement weather—including rain, snow, wind, severe heat, and darkness
·       Travel independently by foot over steep, slick, thickly-vegetated, and broken terrain safely for long distances (up to 5 miles) while carrying backpacks weighing up to 30 pounds 
·       Lift and/or move 50-150 pounds over short distances (10-25 feet)
·       May include tree-climbing and lowering adult cougars (50-150 pounds) safely to the ground using ropes from trees as much as 40 feet above ground level (training will be provided)  
·       Walk, stand, sit, crawl, kneel, climb, and stoop for long periods (up to several hours)
·       Use hands and fingers to manipulate field and office equipment quickly and efficiently 
·       Communicate effectively in spoken and written English
·       Specific vision abilities required by this job include: close vision, distance vision, color vision, peripheral vision, depth perception, and the ability to adjust focus.
·       Operate vehicles safely in field situations including rough and slick forestry roads 
 EMPLOYMENT CONDITIONS
 The Personnel Policies and Procedures of the Swinomish Indian Tribal Community apply to all employees.  The position of Wildlife Field Biologist is considered to be an exempt “standard hour” position.  All offers of employment are contingent on the successful completion of a drug and alcohol screening.  If the position involves regular contact with or control over children or elders, then a successful applicant must also meet minimum standards of character based on a criminal background check.

TRIBAL PREFERENCE
 

INDIAN PREFERENCE APPLIES TO THIS RECRUITMENT

 Employee:      ________________________________        Date:            _____________________
                        [Incumbent], Wildlife Field Biologist
  Supervisor:    ________________________________        Date:            _____________________
                        Brandon Nickerson, Senior Wildlife Biologist
  Director:        ________________________________        Date:            _____________________
                        Tino Villaluz, Program Manager

Download an Application at http://www.swinomish-nsn.gov/resources/human-resources.aspx or request an Application Packet by phone at (360) 466-7347 or e-mail to aiedwards@swinomish.nsn.us or grendon@swinomish.nsn.us. 
Include a cover letter identifying why you feel you are qualified for this position.
Complete the Swinomish Tribal Community application form; do not write, “see resume on the application”.
By 8:00am Pacific Time on Monday March 30, 2026, email completed application, cover letter, and CV/resume as a single PDF document to BOTH:
 Brandon Nickerson (Senior Wildlife Biologist): bnickerson@swinomish.nsn.us
 AND
 Gissela Rendon (Human Resources):  grendon@swinomish.nsn.us
 ·       Applications may also be mailed but must be RECEIVED by the listed due date: 
 Mail to:
Swinomish Tribal Community
Human Resource Office
11404 Moorage Way
La Conner, WA. 98257

Brandon Nickerson

Description
Salary:  $18,000 per year, monies for a portion of in-state tuition for fall, spring, and summer semesters and some monies for a portion of health insurance

Start Date: August 15, 2026

Last Date to Apply: April 28, 2026

Description: 

I seek an enthusiastic, determined, and self-motivated master’s student to work on northern bobwhite movement and survival during fall and winter in the Rolling Plains of Texas. The project is part of a larger study in Texas to understand the influence of supplemental feeding, predator management, and weather on bobwhite demographics. Specifically, the student’s thesis will be centered on examining relationships between food availability, movement, and survival of bobwhites during fall and winter.  On-site field housing and travel will be provided. 

Qualifications:

Bachelor of Science degree conferred by August 1, 2026, in wildlife management, ecology, or a related discipline. The candidate must be able to work under field conditions that include cold temperatures and remote living conditions. Also, must have the capacity to learn quantitative techniques such as survival analysis and movement models. The ability to assist in hiring and lead a crew of technicians is essential. Preference will be given to applicants with prior experience in capturing and handling wildlife.  The student should also be personable and a team player. A clean driving record is required.

For consideration, please send a cover letter, CV, and unofficial transcripts to the contact email below. In addition, please provide a list of at least 3 references.

Contact Person: Dr. Brad Dabbert

Contact email: brad.dabbert@ttu.edu

Contact
Dr. Brad Dabbert
brad.dabbert@ttu.edu (preferred contact method)

POSITION TITLE: Biologist I (Plants and Biological Soil Crusts)
STATUS: Full time or part time (negotiable), non-exempt, seasonal position (3-month term)
REPORTS TO: Deputy Director
POSITIONS REPORTING TO THIS POSITION: none
LOCATION: Reno, Nevada 
EXPECTED PAY RANGE: $25 / hour
 

Description: This position will work in collaboration with the US Geological Survey (USGS) – Western Ecological Research Center.  The Biologist I position will work with a growing team focused on vegetation and biocrusts within a larger program dedicated to the topic of sagebrush ecosystems. The focus of this position is assisting in the synthesis of information as well as project design and planning. Some analyses and writing may also be expected, with a focus on management and restoration of vascular plants and biological soil crusts. Some field work will be associated with the position. Field work can involve 8-day hitches, camping in remote locations, and participating in safety check in threads. Projects are funded primarily by state and federal partners (e.g., Nevada Dept. of Wildlife, Bureau of Land Management, US Fish and Wildlife Service). 

Study System: Field locations are across the Great Basin and Eastern California. During field work, 8-day hitches in remote locations will typically be required.

The Sagebrush ecosystems of the Great Basin and Eastern California are unique and support a diversity of flora and fauna. With this opportunity, you will get the chance to work closely with endemic species, explore large expanses of public land and participate in recreational activities including camping, hiking, climbing, hot springs, mountain biking, and many more! 

Expectations and Core Accountabilities

Synthesizing plant and biocrust community data.
Assisting with project design and planning.
Assisting with writing papers and reports.
Conducting field work when needed.

Desired expertise/skills 

Minimum Qualifications 

All applicants must possess a valid U.S. driver’s license
Experience with GIS experience as well as R
Previous experience collecting biological data in remote, difficult working environments
 Driving four-wheel drive vehicles on dirt roads 
 Working under challenging weather conditions as are common in the Great Basin, specifically hot-dry conditions, and cold-windy conditions 
Attention to detail, critical thinking on the fly, and ability to make deadlines 
A graduate degree related to natural resources and environmental science


Desired qualifications 

Master’s or PhD degree with on focus on biological soil crusts, in environmental science, Botany, Ecology, or similar field of study
Experience with vegetation in the Great Basin region, especially plant and biocrust identification. 

Benefits

If full time:

Employer paid health, dental, and vision plans.
Employer paid Employee Assistance Program for mental health and personal counseling.
Generous vacation and sick leave.
Pro-deals for outdoor industry.
Employer paid professional development

If part time (between 20-30hr/week):

Employer paid Employee Assistance Program for mental health and personal counseling.
Generous vacation and sick leave.
Pro-deals for outdoor industry.
Employer paid professional development

Working Conditions 

Work will take place in office, lab, outdoor lab, and field settings. Field work can involve exposure to extreme temperature and weather conditions, including long hours standing, bending, walking, and performing tasks that require physical exertion. Incumbents may be required to lift containers weighing up to 35 pounds in the performance of the assignment. Incumbents will be expected to work 40 hours/week. 

Contact Information: To apply please send a resume and cover letter to Dr. Jessica Abbott at jessica.abbott@walkerbasin.org, with Biologist I in the subject line. 

Contact
Jessica Abbott
jessica.abbott@walkerbasin.org (preferred contact method)

Description
 *Applications will be reviewed on a rolling-basis.

USDA Forest Service Office/Lab and Location: A fellowship opportunity is available with the US Department of Agriculture (USDA) Forest Service (FS) within the Pacific Northwest Research Station (PNWRS) located in Seattle, Washington.

At the heart of the USDA Forest Service's mission is their purpose. Everything they do is intended to help sustain forests and grasslands for present and future generations. Why? Because their stewardship work supports nature in sustaining life. This is the purpose that drives the agency’s mission and motivates their work across the agency. It’s been there from the agency’s very beginning, and it still drives them. To advance the mission and serve their purpose, the USDA Forest Service balances the short and long-term needs of people and nature by: working in collaboration with communities and our partners; providing access to resources and experiences that promote economic, ecological, and social vitality; connecting people to the land and one another; and delivering world-class science, technology and land management.

Research Project: Wildland fire and smoke are one of the most pervasive hazards to human health and infrastructure in North America.  Prescribed burning is a critical management tool for building community resilience by reducing hazardous levels of fuels, and key for restoring and maintaining fire-adapted ecosystems. To be safe and effective, prescribed burns must be conducted in a specific set of weather and fuel conditions that are conducive to the burn plan’s particular fire behavior and smoke management objectives.  Increasing the pace and scale of prescribed burning at the scale needed to mitigate the evolving threats of wildland fire and smoke will require optimizing our ability to utilize all available resources.

Our group develops decision support systems that harness historical weather and fuel record knowledge, and their counterpart real-time forecasts, to reduce hurdles to increasing prescribed burning. A current focus is on optimizing the identification and utilization of burn-weather windows. We also utilize our data warehouse to investigate the role that weather variability plays in controlling prescribed burn weather opportunity and wildland fire and smoke activity.

We are looking for fellows with interest in weather/fuel and related data processing, analysis and visualization, to help research and develop potential expansions to our suite of weather-knowledge-based decision support systems and tools.

Learning Objectives: The Fellow will learn how wildland fire managers use weather and fuel data to plan and conduct prescribed burns.  The Fellow will gain understanding of the range of meteorological data sources currently available, and their potential applicability and limitations for wildland fire management decision making.  The Fellow will learn to add value to source data by processing it into readily-available, fit-for-purpose products and decision support systems. They will also learn about current needs for additional weather and fuel information and how to expand incorporation of meteorological forecasts into these products and systems to meet them.  

Mentor: The mentor for this opportunity is Andrew Chiodi (andy.chiodi@noaa.gov). If you have questions about the nature of the research, please contact the mentor.

Anticipated Appointment Start Date: April 2026.  Start date is flexible and will depend on a variety of factors.

Appointment Length: The appointment will initially be for one year but may be extended upon recommendation of USDA Forest Service and is contingent on the availability of funds.

Level of Participation: Full time participation is preferred, with part-time opportunity considered for current students.

Participant Stipend: The participant will receive a monthly stipend commensurate with educational level and experience. The anticipated stipend range is $70,000 - $90,000 annually.

Citizenship Requirements: This opportunity is available to U.S. citizens only.

ORISE Information: This program, administered by ORAU through its contract with the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) to manage the Oak Ridge Institute for Science and Education (ORISE), was established through an interagency agreement between DOE and USDA Forest Service. Participants do not become employees of USDA, USDA Forest Service, DOE or the program administrator, and there are no employment-related benefits. Proof of health insurance is required for participation in this program. Health insurance can be obtained through ORISE.

Questions: Please visit our Program Website. After reading, if you have additional questions about the application process please email ORISE.USFS.PNWRS@orau.org and include the reference code for this opportunity.

Qualifications
The qualified candidate should be currently pursuing or have received an associate's, bachelor's, or master's degree in the one of the relevant fields. Degree must have been received within the past five years, or anticipated to be received by 7/1/2028.

Preferred skills:

Programming language appropriate for data analysis and visualization, such as Python,

Description
Field Operations Technician (Full-Time) -- Fayette County, Texas
 Who We Are
 
Land Within Reach is combining conservation and land development in a whole new way in Texas. Wildlife Communities™ are private, large-acreage homestead communities where families reserve 80% of their land for professional wildlife habitat conservation and design their ideal family lifestyle on the remaining 20%.
 
We're looking for a motivated, early-career outdoorsman to join our field operations team and help make Wildlife Communities™ a reality. If you're a recent graduate with a degree in Rangeland, Wildlife and Fisheries Management, Ecology and Conservation Biology, or a related conservation field, you love working outdoors, you're a quick learner with strong work ethic, and you want to be part of meaningful conservation work from the ground up, this role could be perfect for you. If you're all that and you love the native wildlife and epic landscapes that make Texas legendary, you should absolutely give this role a hard look.
 Why This Role Matters
 
You'll be the on-the-ground presence that transforms raw properties into complete Wildlife Communities™—beautiful, functional homestead communities for people and wildlife. This means native grassland management, wildlife habitat restoration, invasive species control, and conservation work alongside property maintenance, repairs, and infrastructure improvements. You're not just maintaining what exists; you're helping build something from the ground up that families will purchase, build within, and call home for generations.
 
Your work will directly affect:
 Conservation outcomes through professional habitat restoration and wildlife management
Development success by producing inventory worthy of our clients' expectations and investment 
Sales success by ensuring properties are always pristine and tour-ready for prospects
Resident satisfaction by maintaining exceptional community standards
Company growth by establishing field operations excellence that supports expanding to multiple communities

This is a foundation-building role with clear growth trajectory. You'll start with foundational equipment (zero-turn mower, UTV, hand tools) and grow into advanced machinery (UTV attachments, track loader, tractor, excavator) as your skills and our operations expand. You'll begin with grounds maintenance and basic conservation work, then progressively learn native prairie seeding, development project coordination, and advanced land management as we expand to multiple communities. As we expand and your skills develop, you'll also support infrastructure improvements and development projects.
 What You'll Do
 
You'll perform on-site field operations across our Wildlife Communities™ in Fayette County, handling everything from conservation work to property maintenance.
 
Native Grassland & Habitat Management
 Learn proper techniques for native prairie seeding operations according to our grassland management plan
Learn proper techniques for invasive species control and habitat management
Spot-treat invasive grasses in alignment with our grassland management plan
Repair feral hog damage to native grasslands including reseeding disturbed areas
Fire ant treatment in alignment with our wildlife management plan
Assist in developing whitetail deer census and management protocols in coordination with conservation professionals
Assist with installation and maintenance of wildlife water features
Support conservation monitoring including data collection and maintenance of bird listening devices
Document wildlife observations (species, numbers, locations, timing) to support sales tours and annual wildlife management reporting
Attend workshops through Texas A&M Natural Resources Institute, Wildlife Habitat Federation, Native Prairies Association of Texas, Plateau Land & Wildlife Management, Texas Parks & Wildlife, and other conservation organizations
Progressively take on more sophisticated conservation work as capabilities grow
 
Property Maintenance & Care
 Maintain neat and orderly appearance of entrance areas, road frontage, fence lines, common areas, and infrastructure
Monitor and fill water tanks for wildlife management and landscaping
General property upkeep: signage cleaning, debris removal, minor repairs as needed
Maintain tour-ready presentation of properties—prospects visit regularly

Equipment Operation & Maintenance
 Operate and maintain zero-turn mower, UTV with attachments, and hand equipment
Perform routine equipment maintenance: oil changes, blade sharpening, cleaning, storage
Identify repair needs and coordinate service with vendors
Maintain field office facility and equipment storage
Ensure all equipment is clean, fueled, and ready for use
 
Important Note: This position involves working with herbicides and other chemical treatments for invasive species control and wildlife management. You must be willing to work safely with these materials following all proper safety protocols and regulations.
 What We're Looking For
 
We're looking for someone with a strong work ethic, solid character, and a desire to learn.
 
Essential Qualities
 Strong work ethic and self-motivation—this role requires working independently
Intelligence and learning agility—you'll be learning new skills constantly
Attention to detail and pride in quality work
Reliability and accountability—our team and residents depend on you
Professional demeanor when representing Land Within Reach to residents, prospects, and contractors
Comfortable working outdoors in Texas weather (heat, rain, humidity)
Physically capable of manual labor and equipment operation
 
Education & Experience
 Bachelor's degree in Rangeland, Wildlife and Fisheries Management, Ecology and Conservation Biology, or related conservation field (recent graduates welcome)
Interest in conservation, native habitat restoration, and land stewardship
Experience with equipment operation helpful but not required—we'll train
 Valid driver's license required
 
Bonus Qualifications
 Farm or ranch experience with livestock or land management
Hands-on experience with native Texas plants and wildlife
Previous work in landscaping or grounds maintenance
Spanish language skills
 What We Offer
 
Compensation & Benefits
 Base Salary: $41,600 annually ($20/hour equivalent)
Operations Bonus Pool: Annual bonus opportunity when the Operations team meets net revenue targets
Health Benefits: Company contribution toward health insurance
Generous PTO Policy
Professional Development: Company-paid attendance at conservation workshops and training programs
 
Growth Trajectory
 Learn professional conservation techniques from experienced contractors and conservation organizations, building relationships with leading Texas conservation professionals as you develop land management expertise across multiple Wildlife Communities™ as we expand
Progress from basic equipment operation to advanced machinery (tractors, track loader, excavator)
Learn development project coordination and management skills
Clear path to Field Operations Supervisor role as company scales
 
Work Environment
 Field-based work at our Fayette County Wildlife Community™ on beautiful native Texas landscape in the heart of Central Texas 
Company shirts provided (must be worn in field) 
Schedule: Full-time, with flexibility based on seasonal demands and weather 
Onsite Work Only: This is hands-on field work requiring daily on-site presence
Location Requirement: Must be living within 45 minutes of La Grange, Texas, or willing to relocate to this area

What Your First Year Looks Like
 
Months 1-3: Foundation Building
 Master equipment operation and basic grounds maintenance procedures
Learn Land Within Reach's Wildlife Communities™ model—from our conservation mission and target market to community layout and quality standards
Establish equipment maintenance routines
Begin invasives control, water monitoring, and road frontage and fence line maintenance responsibilities
 
Months 4-12: Expanding Capabilities
 Progress to more complex equipment operation and larger implements
Begin learning invasive species management techniques
Learn from conservation contractors during project work
Attend conservation workshops and training programs
Assist with native prairie seeding operations according to management plan
Take increasing ownership of routine maintenance with minimal supervision
Handle property maintenance assignments independently
Begin handling development project work assignments
Demonstrate readiness for expanded responsibilities in Year 2
 How to Apply
 
Please submit your resume and a brief cover letter via email to apply@landwithinreach.com with the subject line
"Attn: Field Operations Technician".
 
In your cover letter, please address:
 Why conservation and land stewardship work appeals to you
Your experience with equipment operation, outdoor work, or land management (even if informal)
Why you're interested in starting your career with a small, mission-driven company
 Your willingness to learn and grow into increasing responsibilities
 
Land Within Reach is building something new in Texas—Wildlife Communities™ that prove conservation and upscale country living can work together. This role offers you the opportunity to be part of that from the beginning, learn from experienced professionals, and build a career in conservation land management.
  Contact
Jacob McLaughlin
5126533941
apply@landwithinreach.com (preferred contact method)

Description
About the Project
Woody encroachment is one of the most significant and rapidly intensifying drivers of grassland loss in western US rangelands. In response, conservation leaders across the region – spanning federal/state government, NGOs, industry, university, and private landowner collaboratives – have identified a critical gap in workforce development, training, and technical knowledge needed to implement new planning and design protocols that reduce vulnerability to woody encroachment.
 This position supports a major, multi-year initiative to build that missing workforce capacity. Funded through the NRCS Working Lands for Wildlife, the project will establish a state-of-the-art training and tech-transfer program that will increase the skills of conservation practitioners, landowners, and partner organizations to deliver conservation solutions at scales that matter for working lands conservation.

Position Overview
The Woody Encroachment Coordinator will develop scientific content and curriculum, support multi-partner engagement and outreach planning, and lead logistics for trainings and technical transfer activities. The Coordinator will work closely with a collaborative team of scientists, government agencies, NGOs, and industry to coordinate efforts to improve workforce capacity to improve management of woody encroachment across Great Plains grasslands. 

Key Responsibilities
·       Lead delivery of a multi-faceted woody encroachment outreach and training program.
·       Coordinate the development, organization, and deployment of a new short course and 2.5-day field bootcamp on woody encroachment.
·       Prepare instructional materials, organize learning modules, and integrate content from local experts, university scientists, NRCS, Pheasants Forever, state agencies, and other partners to customize field exercises and classroom activities.
·       Track participant outcomes, maintain budgets, track progress toward project deliverables, and prepare materials for grant reporting.
·       Interpret training results and assist in preparing public-facing summaries, factsheets, learning modules, and digital media.
·       Attend regional partner meetings, training events, field days, and workshops to support implementation of new guidance.
·       Support partners in applying course materials to in-state trainings, ranch practicums, grazing schools, and other outreach events.

Qualifications
Required
·       Master’s degree (Ph.D. preferred) in natural resources, rangeland ecology, environmental science, fire ecology, or a related field
·       Demonstrated knowledge of woody encroachment, Great Plains rangeland systems, and applied grassland conservation
·       Willingness to travel periodically across the Great Plains for courses and workshops
·       Strong written and verbal communication skills
 Preferred
·       Experience developing or delivering trainings, curriculum, workshops, or technical guidance for conservation practitioners
·       Familiarity with extension, NRCS planning processes, and tech-transfer programs 
·       Experience coordinating multi-partner programs
·       Ability to synthesize and communicate complex ecological information to diverse audiences of practitioners
·       Experience in program coordination, logistics, or project management 

Why Join Us
This position sits at the forefront of Great Plains conservation, supporting one of the region’s most urgent and widely recognized ecological challenges – woody encroachment. This position will also play a central role in strengthening the rangeland workforce, co-producing meaningful education and training materials with leading practitioners, and helping partners reduce vulnerability across millions of acres of working lands. This is an opportunity to shape how the next generation of conservation professionals plan, design, and implement strategies to maintain resilient, functioning grassland ecosystems.

To Apply
All applications need to be submitted through the University of Nebraska Employment Website: https://employment.unl.edu/postings/99639
Contact
Dirac Twidwell
dirac.twidwell@unl.edu (preferred contact method)

Description
The Wildlife Conservation Specialist (Summits Operative Team) will be a key member of the Wildlife Restoration Foundation (WRF) team designing and implementing the North American Summit on Collaborating with Wildlife (CW) in Park Gateways. The Wildlife Conservation Specialist (“Wild Spec”) will play vital roles in designing, planning, recruiting content and participants, and implementing the CW Summit in 2026. Depending on individual talents and experience, the Wild Spec will work closely with the WRF President, Director of Operations, Program Managers and/or Project Coordinators one on or both Summits to ensure smooth execution of the Summit methodology with sufficient resources, ample participation, and quality results. Please visit www.wildlifeandparks.org and www.nabsummit.org.
 The Summits are organized by WRF in partnership with dozens of organizations, government agencies, Tribes, and businesses. The Summits are politically neutral and a place where practitioners and researchers share and assess active and conceptual Models and Methodologies (M&Ms) connected to priority themes. The proven Workgroup Dialogue mechanism enables these M&Ms to be informed, strengthened, and replicated as attending practitioners gain new partners and resources. 
 The North American Summit on Collaborating with Wildlife in Park Gateways(CW Summit) vision is the rebuilding of a lost legacy of abundant, healthy and genetically-diverse populations of native North American wildlife thriving amidst human communities bordering park. Note that “Park” includes all kinds of areas set aside for wildlife, and can be federal, state, county, city or private parkland and waters including marine protected areas.
The CW Summit is co-hosted by the Oceans & Wildlife Institute (OWI) and WRF and will take place February 2027  in Corpus Christi, Texas. 
  The WRF Wildlife Conservation Specialist (Wild Spec), in collaboration with WRF leadership, will select and/or be assigned various Summit tasks based on experience, talents and interests. These might be related to substantive content, outreach, logistics, Summit resources, and onsite implementation. Summit team members are responsible for identifying invitees, Models & Methodologies in their knowledge areas, and fundraising (which might be accomplished by getting colleagues to register or recruiting vendors and Supporting Partners). 
 Once joining the Summits Operative Team, the Wild Spec should designate 12-16 hours a week to WRF for a term of at least 3 months. While serving as a volunteer for this time period, the Wild Spec who produces regular, tangible results will be eligible to earn a modest stipend thereafter and will be invited with travel funded to join onsite for Summit prep and proceedings. The WRF Wild Spec role is intended for an individual who takes initiative and can work independently, and is ambitious, creative, attentive, and dedicated to WRF’s mission and the Summit visions. 
 Key Position Responsibilities:
·       Work with the WRF Executive and Summit Operative Team to design and implement various aspects of each Summit
Manage tasks of a CW Summit sub-team (leadership opportunity)
Expand and diversify Summit participant registrations 
Assist with pursuing and summarizing relevant Models & Methodologies that form the Summit program and e-Workbook 
Contribute tangibly to Summit fundraising by achieving results that include the recruitment of attendee, exhibitors, and sponsors attributable to your efforts
Meet deadlines along Summit timelines and contribute a minimum of 20 unique registrant candidates to the Summit invitee database each week upon request.
 Education & Experience:
Bachelor's degree in science, agriculture, business, or field related to Summit themes; Master’s degree a plus
Proven experience in planning and executing large-scale events or conferences
Strong organizational and time management skills
Excellent communication and interpersonal abilities
Tech proficiency, with Canva, Wix and/or Whova talents a plus 
Background in wildlife, land or marine conservation and/or ranching ideal
Passion for the WRF mission and Summit visions required!
 The Wildlife Conservation Specialist will report to the WRF President, Director of Operations, and/or a Program Manager or Project Coordinator, depending on the task. Hours are flexible within the week, but participation in biweekly Summit Operative Team and (as relevant) vendor/venue meetings via Zoom is required.

To apply: Send a short (less than one page) cover letter and resume with 2 references to info@wildlifeandparks.org. Please name the electronic file sent in this format: [Date]_WildSpec_[Your Name]. Please put “[your name] + Wild Spec Candidate” in the subject line of the email.
 Thank you for your interest in contributing your skills and ambition to the Wildlife Restoration Foundation team!
Contact
Sydney Payne
703-300-0025
info@wildlifeandparks.org (preferred contact method)

Description
Description:
 One research technician is needed for 3 months (May – July, start and end flexible with semester dates) to support a graduate research project in the Forestry Program at Southern Illinois University. The technician will provide field and office support for a project focused on using acoustic recording units (ARUs) to study eastern whip-poor-will in the Central Hardwoods Forest, among other species. Specific duties will include: 1) deploying acoustic recorders on public land (70% of duties); 2) conducting nocturnal surveys for nightjars (15%); 3) data entry and processing (10%); 4) assisting with other forest bird surveys as needed (5%). Field work in Indiana will take place May 11– mid July.
 Field housing is provided by Purdue University/Indiana DNR at Yellowwood State Forest. Basic utilities are included. Residents will be required to pay a $300 refundable security deposit prior to beginning work in addition to a $20 non-refundable cleaning fee. The field house is 20 minutes from Bloomington, IN, and 15 minutes from Nashville, Indiana. 
  Qualifications
 The successful applicant will (1) have or be working towards a B.S. degree in wildlife, ecology, biology, forestry, zoology, or related fields; (2) have a strong work ethic with ability to work independently as well as part of a small research team; (3) be comfortable working at night in forested conditions, (4) a valid US driver’s license without restrictions, (5) ability to conduct independent field work in difficult conditions (e.g., hot, humid, very steep terrain, poison ivy, ticks, venomous snakes), during day and night in sometimes isolated areas; (6) have excellent communication skills with peers, agency staff, and members of the public; and (7) be highly dependable with a positive disposition. 

To apply please email Haley Holiman (haley.holiman@siu.edu) the following as a single pdf with the email heading “Forest Bird Tech”. Applications will be reviewed as they are received. 
 1. A cover letter that describes past experiences and career goals 
 2. A resume/CV
 3. Contact information for 3 references
  Contact
Haley Holiman
haley.holiman@siu.edu (preferred contact method)

Description
BAT ECOLOGY, CONSERVATION, & MANAGEMENT METHODS 

COURSE DESCRIPTION -  The conservation of endangered bat species requires an understanding of their unique physiological ecology. Understanding the suite of adaptations that enable bats to occupy their niche provides valuable insight into their demographics, which is a central component of any effective management strategy. This course introduces students to these fascinating and complex mammals. In addition to learning about bat phylogenetics, anatomy, and physiology, which in turn informs their life history and the ecological roles they perform within ecosystems, the course also covers the major threats to bats, basic concepts and protocols for monitoring bat populations in temperate regions, and current strategies for population stabilization and recovery. 
 DATES
July 6 - October 11, 2026

Description
Natural Resources Specialist 

CEHMM is a 501(c)3 non-profit organization that offers a wide verity of environmental services to clients throughout southern New Mexico. Our Environmental Services Department specializes in natural resources assessment and environmental permitting at the federal and state levels. A successful candidate may have experience with plant & wildlife surveys, environmental assessments (EAs), a working knowledge of the NEPA, and environmental planning. 
 The Natural Resource Specialist position is a permanent full-time position located in Carlsbad, NM, reporting to the Environment Services Project Manager. The Natural Resource Specialist will accomplish a wide variety of client needs including but not limited to:
 ·        Conduct natural resource surveys (wildlife, plant, karst, hydrology, etc.)
·        Prepare NEPA documents (Environmental Assessments, Environmental Site Assessments) and related environmental planning or permitting deliverables
·        Assist with environmental planning, conflict analysis, and project coordination
·        Perform technical field surveys (wetland delineations, SWPPP inspections, formal on-site assessments, etc.)
·        Collect, enter, and manage field data; maintain accurate records and mapping outputs
·        Draft technical reports, permit applications, special studies, and presentations
·        Ensure compliance with federal, state, and local natural resource laws and regulations
·        Collaborate with federal, state, and local agencies, private subcontractors, and clients
·        Other duties as assigned 
 The salary range for this position is $25/hr. to $35hr. CEHMM offers a very relaxed work environment with flexible schedules, competitive compensation, benefits including 401K, quality insurance options, relocation reimbursement, education reimbursement, and clothing allowance.
 Minimum Job Requirements: 

Required

·        Bachelor’s degree (B.A. or B.S.) in Environmental Science, Wildlife Science, Ecology, Biology, Geology, or related field
·        Minimum of two years of professional experience in natural resource management, consulting, or environmental permitting
·        Ability to work long hours in the field in variable and challenging conditions
·        Working knowledge of ArcGIS Pro
·        Ability to effectively and clearly communicate with clients, agencies, and team members
·        Possession of a valid driver’s license with a clean driving record
 Preferred                             

·        Master’s degree (MS) in Environmental Science, Wildlife Science, Ecology, Biology, Geology, or related field
·        Experience with NEPA document preparation (EAs, EISs, Environmental Site Assessments)
·        Experience with state and federal environmental permitting processes
·        Experience performing technical environmental surveys (e.g., wetland delineations, SWPPP inspections, cave & karst)
·        Ability to pull a trailer and operate a UTV
 Knowledge, Skills, and Abilities: 

Required

·        Knowledge of natural resource survey methods (wildlife, plant, karst, hydrology, etc.)
·        Ability to collect, analyze, and manage field data accurately
·        Competence in technical writing for environmental reports and planning documents
·        Proficiency with ArcGIS Pro and standard office software (Word, Excel)
·        Ability to communicate clearly and effectively with clients, subcontractors, regulatory agencies, and colleagues
·        Strong organizational skills and attention to detail
 

Preferred

·        Experience applying state and federal natural resource regulations
·        Experience with NEPA document preparation and permitting
·        Familiarity with advanced GIS analysis and mapping
·        Ability to troubleshoot problems in the field and adapt to dynamic conditions

Description
About Us
The Lajuma Research Centre is home to more than 2,500 plant species and 600 tree species. They cover a variety of habitats including woody grasslands, mountain bushveld, mistbelt forest, and riverine forest, and their related floral diversity. Vegetation communities and structures are dynamic and need to be constantly studied. An array of vegetation studies can be undertaken in this unique landscape. 

Internship Overview
Depending on the project, interns will be actively identifying species, delineating environments or sampling vegetation communities, further expanding knowledge on this crucial aspect of ecology. 

Activities may include:
Ground truthing vegetation communities to update vegetation maps
Alien and invasive species removal/mapping
Focal data collection on species utilisation of key vegetation, eg Ficus sp. 
Assisting with data management and reporting

Fieldwork Requirements
All fieldwork is carried out on foot, often over uneven terrain and in variable weather. Students should be comfortable living remotely, hiking, and have a basic level of fitness.

Eligibility
Open to students, recent graduates, or individuals interested in field sampling and conservation
GIS proficiency is beneficial

Duration
A minimum stay of 8 weeks is recommended.

Conservation Fee
A conservation fee is required, which directly supports conservation and research efforts and includes:

Shared accommodation
Pick-up and drop-off from/to Louis Trichardt
Fortnightly town trips for supplies

How to Apply
Send a CV and cover letter to applications@lajuma.com

If unavailable during specified dates, contact us to discuss alternatives.

Vernal, Utah
Tags:
Graduate Opportunities
Undergraduate Opportunities
Description

1 position for the 2026 field season.


Primary duties include assisting regional fisheries biologists with:

Native cutthroat trout enhancement projects throughout Northeast Utah: fish trap/fish spawning operations, stream habitat improvement projects, rotenone treatments, invasive species removals (brook trout), fisheries population and assessment surveys
Completing required training: purchasing, drivers, computer and information technology, pesticide applicators licensing, and helicopter and airplane safety trainings among others will be administered
High mountain lake fish surveys
Wilderness horse pack camping trips
Reservoir sampling including gill netting, fyke netting, boat electrofishing, fish tagging, foodweb/ecology studies
Habitat protection and enhancement activities, trespass cattle monitoring, riparian enclosure/exclosure fencing
Help with conservation outreach activities (e.g., fishing clinics/free fishing day)
Assist biologists with crew training and supervision. Crew leads will be expected to keep subordinate employees productive and on task in an independent capacity. Crew will be assigned equipment upkeep and maintenance duties while not in field duty status.
Other duties may be assigned on occasion

Much of this work is completed in remote areas requiring frequent overnight camping in some of the most scenic areas of Utah (often no cell service). Campers, food, and supplies are provided on overnight trips. Housing is provided, benefits are not provided for this position. Work related gear (waders, wader boots, uniform t-shirts and hat) is provided.


Qualifications: Progress toward a fisheries management or related college degree is required. This position is geared toward undergrads or recent graduates seeking additional experience in the fisheries management field.


Experience with: electrofishing and netting surveys, working, camping, and hiking in remote environments, habitat and/or landscaping, fish handling, maintaining fisheries sampling gears, working/leading in team environments, horse riding, and identification of trout species will be used as ranking criteria. Hard work ethic, critical thinking skills, ability to work independently, and attention to detail is a must. Candidates are expected to be able to pack 50 pounds through mountainous terrain for 8+ miles in a day, often in slippery, rocky terrain.

HOW TO APPLY
Apply by sending cover letter, resume, and 3 references to Carsen Hinkhouse: chinkhouse@utah.gov

Questions? 435-828-3317


When you apply, please indicate that you are responding to the posting on Texas AM job board.
Contact
Carsen Hinkhouse
435-828-3317
chinkhouse@utah.gov (preferred contact method)

Title: Lab Technician (DNA Extraction/Soil Microbial Analyses)

Classification: Temporary/Seasonal Positions 

Agency: Mississippi State University

Job Description:  

This is a 3- to 4-month seasonal position working with Mississippi State University. The selected applicant will be conducting lab analyses of fecal and soil samples for the MSU Deer Lab. The selected individual will be trained to run the analyses in the lab and work independently once comfortable with the process. Position duties include but are not limited to: (1) Conducting DNA extractions on deer fecal pellets without contaminating the samples, (2) operating lab equipment such as bead-beaters, centrifuges, vortexes, pipets, etc., and (3) following step-by-step protocols for both DNA extractions and soil microbial analyses. The position start date is still pending, but likely the 18th of May.

Location: Mississippi State University; Starkville, Mississippi

Qualifications: 

Applicants must have a bachelor’s degree or be currently enrolled in an undergraduate or graduate program in wildlife biology, ecology, or a related discipline. Applicants MUST be able to work for the full duration of the project. Preference will be given to candidates with previous lab experience in DNA or microbial analyses. 

Applicants must be able to keep detailed records of lab work and follow DNA extraction protocols. The selected candidate must abide by safety guidelines and keep a clean workstation. 

To apply, please send a resume and contact information for 3 references as a single PDF file to Logan Maier at ljm383@msstate.edu with “DNA Lab Extraction Technician Application” in the subject line. For questions, email Logan Maier at ljm383@msstate.edu. 

Position will be filled as a suitable candidate is found 

Start date:  Pending, but employment will include May-August 2026, possibly extending into September

Application deadline:   April 15th or until position is filled

  Contact
Mississippi State Deer Lab
ljm383@msstate.edu (preferred contact method)

Description
CEHMM a 501(c)3 non-profit conservation and environmental services organization, is seeking a full-time Range Conservation Scientist to develop technical standards and specifications to manage, improve, and protect natural resources to maximize their use while safeguarding the environment. CEHMM provides practical technical support in range research efforts and in the scientific management, protection, and development of grasslands and other range resources. 
 The Range Conservation Scientist position is a permanent full-time position located in Carlsbad, NM, reporting to the District I Conservation Project Manager. The Range Conservation Scientist will accomplish a wide variety of client needs including but not limited to:
 ·         Develop, implement, and review grazing plans, rangeland improvement projects, and habitat management practices
·         Conduct vegetation, soil, and hydrology surveys; analyze rangeland health conditions
·         Study forage plant communities, grazing patterns, and rangeland productivity
·         Provide technical guidance to landowners and agencies on grazing management, compliance, and range improvements
·         Compile, analyze, and manage rangeland monitoring data; produce GIS-based maps
·         Prepare technical reports, conservation plans, and research summaries
·         Ensure compliance with regulatory requirements and CEHMM protocols
·         Collaborate with federal and state agencies, ranchers, and industry partners to improve rangeland health
·         Provide technical support to project managers and other conservation staff
·         Other duties as assigned  
 The salary range for this position is $58,656 - $90,000 annually. CEHMM offers a very relaxed work environment with flexible schedules, competitive compensation, benefits including 401K, quality insurance options, relocation reimbursement, education reimbursement, and clothing allowance.
 Minimum Job Requirements: 

Required

·         Bachelor’s degree in Range Science, Rangeland Ecology, Natural Resource Management, or a closely related field
·         Minimum of five years of professional experience in rangeland management, grazing management, or habitat conservation
·         Valid driver’s license and a clean driving record
·         Ability to perform strenuous fieldwork in variable and extreme conditions
 Preferred                             

Master’s degree in Range Science, Rangeland Ecology, Natural Resource Management, or a closely related field
Over five years of professional experience in range conservation, grazing systems, or natural resource management
Proficiency with ArcGIS Pro for mapping and data visualization
Familiarity with NEPA processes, permitting, or land management agreements


Familiarity with sensitive species conservation programs (e.g., candidate conservation agreements or habitat conservation plans)
 Knowledge, Skills, and Abilities: 

Required
Advanced knowledge of range ecology, grazing management systems, and rangeland monitoring techniques
Ability to develop and evaluate grazing plans and habitat improvement projects
Proficiency in technical writing for conservation plans and scientific reports
Ability to conduct and analyze vegetation, soil, and rangeland condition surveys
Ability to work independently, set priorities, and ensure accuracy in field and office tasks
Strong communication skills for working effectively with landowners, agencies, and partner organizations
 Preferred
Proficiency with ArcGIS for mapping and analysis
Ability to present technical findings to stakeholders, agencies, and professional groups
Experience mentoring junior staff or field technicians

Description
 *Applications are reviewed on a rolling-basis.

ARS Office/Lab and Location: A research opportunity is currently available with the U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA), Agricultural Research Service (ARS), located in Davis, California.

The Agricultural Research Service (ARS) is the U.S. Department of Agriculture's chief scientific in-house research agency with a mission to find solutions to agricultural problems that affect Americans every day from field to table. ARS will deliver cutting-edge, scientific tools and innovative solutions for American farmers, producers, industry, and communities to support the nourishment and well-being of all people; sustain our nation’s agroecosystems and natural resources; and ensure the economic competitiveness and excellence of our agriculture. The vision of the agency is to provide global leadership in agricultural discoveries through scientific excellence.

The Pollinator Health Laboratory in Davis, CA is located in the heart of the Central Valley and surrounded by agricultural lands, providing easy access to commercial beekeepers and realistic field settings to maintain experimental apiaries. Simultaneously, the facility contains state of the art instruments and equipment for performing complex arthropod bioassays as well as physiological and molecular analyses.

Research Project: The pollinator health laboratory is currently developing and testing novel tools and strategies to control Varroa destructor, a highly destructive parasite of honey bee colonies.  The participant will be a part of this by performing bioassays on Varroa mites and honey bees, recording data, collecting samples, and processing and conducting various molecular analyses on the collected samples. This will be in both the laboratory and field using modern molecular techniques.

Learning Objectives: The participant will develop the ability to evaluate and enhance the health of honey bees and their colonies by performing a range of relevant tests and activities. This will include selecting, developing, and, when necessary, modifying methods and procedures to best meet specific research goals. The participant will also learn to plan, implement, and maintain laboratory bioassays involving honey bees at various developmental stages and Varroa mites obtained from field colonies. In addition, they will adapt and apply techniques to study the physiological and biochemical responses of honey bees and Varroa mites to different treatments and processes, as well as analyze samples collected from both field and laboratory experiments.

Mentor(s): The mentor for this opportunity is Julia Fine (julia.fine@usda.gov). If you have questions about the nature of the research, please contact the mentor(s).

Anticipated Appointment Start Date: May 1, 2026. Start date is flexible and will depend on a variety of factors.

Appointment Length: The appointment will initially be for one year, but may be renewed upon recommendation of ARS and is contingent on the availability of funds.

Level of Participation: The appointment is full time.

Participant Stipend: The participant will receive a monthly stipend commensurate with educational level and experience. The anticipated stipend range is $5,000 - $5,702 monthly.

Citizenship Requirements: This opportunity is available to U.S. citizens only.

ORISE Information: This program, administered by ORAU through its contract with the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) to manage the Oak Ridge Institute for Science and Education (ORISE), was established through an interagency agreement between DOE and ARS. Participants do not become employees of USDA, ARS, DOE or the program administrator, and there are no employment-related benefits. Proof of health insurance is required for participation in this program. Health insurance can be obtained through ORISE.

Questions: Please visit our Program Website. After reading, if you have additional questions about the application process, please email ORISE.ARS.PacificWest@orau.org and include the reference code for this opportunity.

Qualifications
The qualified candidate should have received a master's degree in one of the relevant fields. Degree must have been received within the past five years.

Preferred skills:

Entomology and honey bee biology and physiology.
Experience with and being around honey bee colonies, proficiency with basic molecular techniques such as qPCR, and experience conducting arthropod bioassays and performing statistical analyses to generate preliminary results.
Experience handling Varroa mites and in vitro rearing of honey bees.

Description
The Atlanta Botanical Garden invites applications for the Conservation Horticulture Intern position. This short-term internship opportunity aims to host students and recent graduates from universities in Northeast Georgia. Interns will gain immersive experience in conservation horticulture, habitat restoration, and seed banking while building professional networks that can lead to future academic, research, and career opportunities. Successful candidates will work under the supervision of the Conservation Safeguarding Nursery Manager.

Conservation Horticulture:
The Garden maintains extensive ex situ collections of imperiled species native to the southeastern continental United States. These collections require daily maintenance by specialized staff. Horticultural work will take place at the Conservation Safeguarding Nursery in Gainesville, GA. Interns will work directly with Conservation Horticulture staff to focus on the propagation, cultivation, and curation of threatened and endangered plants.

Native Plant Materials Production for Restoration:
Conservation Horticulture Interns will also assist in developing a native plant materials production program to establish a supply chain of high-quality seeds and plugs for restoration. This work includes propagating and maintaining rare and associated common wetland species from southeastern Appalachia and Florida panhandle wet prairies. Interns will work directly with the Conservation Safeguarding Manager and Conservation Seed Increase Horticulturist in greenhouse production, establishment and maintenance of a seed increase farm, and operation of a small restoration seed bank.

SUMMARY:
Administers a wildlife damage program, conducts crop appraisal, prescribes abatement, investigates livestock kills, conducts pesticide applications and works closely with state/federal/local agencies.

Responsible for planning, organizing, and conducting direct control operations to reduce wildlife damage within a geographical area or technical/subject area of program involvement.
Inspects and surveys areas and, as necessary, will revise existing WDM programs.
Uses WDM tools/devices which may include some or all of the following depending on need in assigned area: firearms; foothold, body-grip, cage traps; foot snares; neck snares; chemical control methods; pyrotechnics; noise cannons/exploders; drop nets; rocket/cannon nets; various other scaring devices.
Correctly identifies current, and forecasts future, damage problems and relates them to the damage-causing species.
Independently determines and selects the most effective means of control for individual situations.

The use of firearms, traps, snares, pesticides, immobilizing drugs, pyrotechnics, electronic harassment devices, and other non-lethal control tools.

ARS Office/Lab and Location: A postdoctoral research opportunity is currently available with the U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA), Agricultural Research Service (ARS), Stuttgart National Aquatics Research Center in Dr. Michael Deshotel's laboratory, located in Stuttgart, Arkansas.

The Agricultural Research Service (ARS) is the U.S. Department of Agriculture's chief scientific in-house research agency with a mission to find solutions to agricultural problems that affect Americans every day from field to table. ARS will deliver cutting-edge, scientific tools and innovative solutions for American farmers, producers, industry, and communities to support the nourishment and well-being of all people; sustain our nation’s agroecosystems and natural resources; and ensure the economic competitiveness and excellence of our agriculture. The vision of the agency is to provide global leadership in agricultural discoveries through scientific excellence.

Research Project: The primary focus of the laboratory is the control and prevention of bacterial diseases in warmwater fish, a critical issue for the U.S. aquaculture industry that causes annual economic losses estimated at over $300 million. The research involves using antibiotic alternatives and vaccine development to address this challenge.

The participant will be involved in projects evaluating novel therapeutic compounds, vaccines, and bacteriophages (phages) for the treatment and prevention of bacterial diseases. On-going projects include:

Identification of genes and pathways for disease resistance.
Phage and lysin therapies, including mastering techniques for purifying and amplifying bacteriophages.
Efficacy testing of novel antimicrobials, including conducting in vivo and in vitro studies to evaluate the efficacy and toxicity of novel compounds and phages.
Vaccine development and conducting vaccine trials.
The candidate will collaborate closely with the principal investigator, support scientists, and technicians as part of a multidisciplinary team. They will research both independently and collaboratively, prepare peer-reviewed publications, and create conference presentations.

Learning Objectives: This opportunity offers significant professional development, including exposure to a Federal Research Environment, hands-on scientific experience, and the chance to contribute to high-impact research. The participant will learn to properly design and conduct studies and how to interpret experimental data to evaluate its usefulness for industry stakeholders.

Mentor(s): The mentor for this opportunity is Michael Deshotel (michael.deshotel@usda.gov). If you have questions about the nature of the research, please contact the mentor(s).

Anticipated Appointment Start Date: March 23, 2026. Start date is flexible and will depend on a variety of factors.

Appointment Length: The appointment will initially be for one year, but may be renewed upon recommendation of ARS and is contingent on the availability of funds.

Level of Participation: The appointment is full time.

Participant Stipend: The participant will receive a monthly stipend commensurate with educational level and experience. The anticipated stipend range is $55,000 - $90,000 annually.

Citizenship Requirements: This opportunity is available to U.S. citizens only.

ORISE Information: This program, administered by ORAU through its contract with the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) to manage the Oak Ridge Institute for Science and Education (ORISE), was established through an interagency agreement between DOE and ARS. Participants do not become employees of USDA, ARS, DOE or the program administrator, and there are no employment-related benefits. Proof of health insurance is required for participation in this program. Health insurance can be obtained through ORISE.

Questions: Please visit our Program Website. After reading, if you have additional questions about the application process, please email ORISE.ARS.Southeast@orau.org and include the reference code for this opportunity.

Qualifications
The qualified candidate should have received or be currently pursuing a doctoral degree in one of the relevant fields (Microbiology, Immunology, Physiology, Cell Biology, Biochemistry, Molecular Biology, or a related field in Biological Sciences).

Preferred skills:

In vivo animal vaccines, antibody assays, flow cytometry, and chromatography
Strong immunology and molecular biology background
A record of peer-reviewed publications
Well-versed in standard molecular, biochemical, and immunological techniques, cell culture-based assays, and PCR

Description
 *Applications are reviewed on a rolling-basis.

ARS Office/Lab and Location: A research opportunity is currently available with the U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA), Agricultural Research Service (ARS), located in Aberdeen, Idaho.

The Agricultural Research Service (ARS) is the U.S. Department of Agriculture's chief scientific in-house research agency with a mission to find solutions to agricultural problems that affect Americans every day from field to table. ARS will deliver cutting-edge, scientific tools and innovative solutions for American farmers, producers, industry, and communities to support the nourishment and well-being of all people; sustain our nation’s agroecosystems and natural resources; and ensure the economic competitiveness and excellence of our agriculture. The vision of the agency is to provide global leadership in agricultural discoveries through scientific excellence.

The Small Grains and Potato Germplasm Research Unit in Aberdeen, Idaho develops and releases improved varieties of barley, oat and potato germplasm with improved disease and pest resistance. It also houses the National Small Grains Collection, a component of the National Plant Germplasm System, that maintains genetically diverse collections of wheat, barley, oat, rice, rye and other small grains.

Research Project: The participant will join a barley and oat improvement project, whose goals are to enhance barley and oat productivity, quality, and stress resistance by combing traditional, molecular, and genomics methods. The participant will be part of the research effort in the laboratory, greenhouse, and field environment, and will be involved in creating experimental designs and treatments, collecting experimental data, and analyzing and interpreting results. 

Learning Objectives: Under the guidance of a mentor, the intern will be given the opportunity to participate in preparing plant bacterial colonies of Xanthomonas translucens pv. translucens (Xtt), evaluating barley, oat and wheat germplasm for bacterial leaf streak infection, creating and validating molecular markers specific to Xtt, quantifying bacterial DNA in infected host tissues, and correlating bacterial DNA content with bacterial colonies, all of which will enhance the participant's training and skills.  Attending mentor-approved meetings and workshops will be encouraged.

Mentor(s): The mentor for this opportunity is Belayneh Yimer (belayneh.yimer@usda.gov). If you have questions about the nature of the research, please contact the mentor(s).

Anticipated Appointment Start Date: May 4, 2026.  Start date is flexible and will depend on a variety of factors.

Appointment Length: The appointment will initially be for three months, but may be renewed upon recommendation of ARS and is contingent on the availability of funds.

Level of Participation: The appointment is full time.

Participant Stipend: The participant will receive a monthly stipend commensurate with educational level and experience. The anticipated stipend range is $4,800 - $5,600 monthly.

Citizenship Requirements: This opportunity is available to U.S. citizens only.

ORISE Information: This program, administered by ORAU through its contract with the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) to manage the Oak Ridge Institute for Science and Education (ORISE), was established through an interagency agreement between DOE and ARS. Participants do not become employees of USDA, ARS, DOE or the program administrator, and there are no employment-related benefits. Proof of health insurance is required for participation in this program. Health insurance can be obtained through ORISE.

Questions: Please visit our Program Website. After reading, if you have additional questions about the application process, please email ORISE.ARS.PacificWest@orau.org and include the reference code for this opportunity.

Qualifications
The qualified candidate should be currently pursuing or has received a bachelor's, master's, or doctoral degree in the one of the relevant fields.

Preferred skills:

Relevant coursework in Biology and agricultural sciences.
Experience with molecular biology techniques.
Experience with microorganisms.
Willingness to conduct field, greenhouse and laboratory experiments.
Strong communication skills for collaborative experimental design and reporting results.
Stipend
$4,800.00 – $5,600.00 Monthly

Description
Job Objective
Implementation of wildlife and habitat management techniques that ensure the Ranch maintains natural resources that are sustainable and of optimal habitat for preferred wildlife species.

Essential Job Responsibilities
·             Assist with daily, weekly, and monthly wildlife and habitat management on a private Ranch with occasional travel to other Ranch properties
·            Coordinate annual and monthly plans with other Ranch and Wildlife Managers to reach weekly/monthly/annual goals
·            Assist Area Wildlife Manager planning, developing, implementing, and maintaining waterfowl management projects
·            Assist with all aspects of small scientific deer breeding operation
·            Plan and conduct building upkeep, maintenance, and landscape at Ranch Headquarters
·            Work with Area Wildlife Manager to analyze all wildlife data to create reports, recommendations, etc.
·            Ensure the Ranch and deer breeding operation are adhering to all TPWD regulations.
·            Assist managing various employees.
·            Assist preparing, planning, and implementing prescribed burn, brush and wildlife management plans
·            Maintain wildlife water development across the Ranch
·            Conduct wildlife surveys for deer, quail, turkey, waterfowl, and reptiles as needed
·            Assist with all aspects of wildlife/waterfowl food plots and irrigation/water production systems
·            Collect waterfowl scouting and data records
·            Assist with all other ranch activities as assigned by the Ranch or Wildlife Manager’s to aid in the development process of ranching and wildlife operations   

Other Job Responsibilities
·            Ability to be available 24 hours per day (on call for questions, emergencies, etc.).
·            Maintains employee confidence and protects company assets, including intellectual property, by keeping information confidential.
·            Contributes to team effort by accomplishing related results as needed.

Qualifications
 ·            Ability to clearly and effectively communicate and report to Ranch Manager and Wildlife Manager
·            Willingness to gain experience with mapping software and various computer related work items
·            Must be a team player who works with others toward a common goal
·            Operation and maintenance of heavy equipment
·            Ability to accurately solve problems
·            Ability to effectively communicate with Ranch personnel
·            Ability to work established work schedule
·            Ability to work days/nights/weekends/holidays
·            Ability to establish and maintain effective working relationships with employees, supervisors, other departments, officials, and the public
·            Ability to consider safety in all decisions
·            Ability to complete multiple, diverse tasks of differing priorities
·            Ability to successfully perform the responsibilities of this position
·            Must have knowledge and understanding of basic computer applications including Word, Excel, PowerPoint and any other related software
·            Must develop and master understanding of ranch organizational responsibilities for each Division and abide by all policies
·            Must be a team player, possess excellent communication skills, and have a passion for natural resource management
·            Must have a valid driver’s license in good standing, the ability to be an approved driver for the company and to safely operate vehicles
·            Must have valid Texas hunting license
·            Must have the ability to traverse rough terrain and work independently in harsh conditions   

Education Requirements
·         Prefer a bachelor’s degree in range, wildlife, or an agriculture related field with at least 1 years’ experience or equivalent work experience

* Position includes: competitive salary, bonus, vehicle for work travel, 401K with match, great health insurance 

Please email resume and 3 references to with Assistant Ranch Manager in subject line:

swedenranch@hotmail.com





Contact
Slade Schwenke
swedenranch@hotmail.com (preferred contact method)
